elements.sent-2411-001: We present two models of sparse dynamic networks that display transitivity - the tendency for vertices sharing a common neighbour to be neighbours of one another.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our first network is a continuous time Markov chain $G=\{G_t=(V,E_t), t\ge 0\}$ whose states are graphs with the common vertex set $V=\{1,\dots, n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-003: The transitions are defined as follows.
elements.sent-2411-004: Given $t$, the vertex pairs $\{i,j\}\subset V$ are assigned independent exponential waiting times $A_{ij}$.
elements.sent-2411-005: At time $t+\min_{ij A_{ij}$ the pair $\{i_0,j_0\}$ with $A_{i_0j_0 =\min_{ij A_{ij}$ toggles its adjacency status.}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: To mimic clustering patterns of sparse real networks we set intensities $a_{ij}$ of exponential times $A_{ij}$ to be negatively correlated with the degrees of the common neighbours of vertices $i$ and $j$ in $G_t$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Another dynamic network is based on a latent Markov chain $H=\{H_t=(V\cup W, E_t), t\ge 0\}$ whose states are bipartite graphs with the bipartition $V\cup W$, where $W=\{1,\dots,m\}$ is an auxiliary set of attributes/affiliations.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our second network $G'=\{G'_t =(E'_t,V), t\ge 0\}$ is the affiliation network defined by $H$: vertices $i_1,i_2\in V$ are adjacent in $G'_t$ whenever $i_1$ and $i_2$ have a common neighbour in $H_t$.
elements.sent-2411-009: We analyze geometric properties of both dynamic networks at stationarity and show that networks possess high clustering.
elements.sent-2411-010: They admit tunable degree distribution and clustering coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-011: Many real networks, especially those depicting human interaction, like social networks of friendships, collaboration networks, citation networks and other show clustering, the propensity of nodes to cluster together by forming relatively small groups with a high density of ties within a group.
elements.sent-2411-012: Clustering is closely related to network transitivity, the tendency for two nodes sharing a common neighbour to be neighbors of one another thus forming a triangle of connections.
elements.sent-2411-013: Locally, in a vicinity of a node, this tendency can be quantified by the probability that two randomly selected neighbours of the node are adjacent.
elements.sent-2411-014: The network average of this probability, called the (average) local clustering coefficient, is used to quantify the network transitivity.
elements.sent-2411-015: Another popular measure of network transitivity, the global clustering coefficient, is the probability that two randomly selected neighbours of a randomly selected node are adjacent.
elements.sent-2411-016: In many social networks both clustering coefficients are on the order of tens of persent while the edge density, the probability that two randomly selected nodes are adjacent, is of much smaller order.
elements.sent-2411-017: Often the edge density scales as $n^{-1}$, where $n$ is the number of nodes in the network.
elements.sent-2411-018: We call networks with such edge densities sparse.
elements.sent-2411-019: Mathematical modelling of sparse networks displaying clustering/transitivity has attracted considerable attention in the literature, see e.g., \cite{Hofstad_book_2_2024 and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-020: We briefly review several approaches to modeling of clustered networks.
elements.sent-2411-021: In order to enhance the number of triangles in an evolving locally tree-like network Holme and Kim \cite{Holme_Kim_2002 suggested inserting additional edges that close desired fraction of open triangles (paths of lenght two).}
elements.sent-2411-022: Newman \cite{MEJNewman_2009 generalised the configuration random graph model by prescribing network nodes numbers of triangles they participate in.}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this way a predefined number of triangles can be introduced into configuration random graph.
elements.sent-2411-024: Bollob\'as et al. \cite{Bollobas_Janson_Riordan_2011 built a clustered network by taking a union of randomly located small dense subgraphs of variable sizes.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Guillaume and Latapy \cite{Guillaume_Latapy_2004 noted an underlying bipartite structure present in many social networks, where nodes (actors) sharing a common hobby or affiliation are more likely to become friends, and where each hobby/affiliation defines a tightly connected cluster of actors related to it.}
elements.sent-2411-026: They suggested modelling a clustered network by first linking actors to affiliations and then connecting actors that share common affiliations, see also \cite{B_G_J_K_R_2015_models , \cite{Hofstad_Komjathy_Vadon_2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: We call such networks affiliation networks.
elements.sent-2411-028: The present paper is devoted to the modelling of sparse and clustered dynamic networks using Markov chains.
elements.sent-2411-029: By dynamic network we mean a collection of random graphs $\{G_t=(V,E_t)$, $t\ge 0\}$ sharing the same vertex set $V=\{1,\dots, n\}$ and having random edge sets $E_t$, $t\ge 0$.
elements.sent-2411-030: We present two stationary random processes $\{G_t, t\ge 0\}$ with tunable degree distribution and tunable non-vanishing clustering coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our study is build upon earlier work on dynamic network Markov chains \cite{Grindrod_Higham_Parsons_2012 , \cite{UzupyteWit_2020 , \cite{Zhang_Moore_Newman_2017 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-033: The network admits tunable edge density and degree distribution, but since the edges are inserted/deleted independently of each other it does not show clustering.
elements.sent-2411-034: Grindrod et al \cite{Grindrod_Higham_Parsons_2012 and Užupytė and Witt \cite{UzupyteWit_2020 introduced transitivity into the network Markov chain by relating the birth/death rate of an edge to the number of triangles it participates in (cf.. \cite{Holme_Kim_2002 ).}}}

elements.sent-2411-037: A drawback of the models of \cite{Grindrod_Higham_Parsons_2012 , \cite{UzupyteWit_2020 is that for large $n$ they have a little control over the edge density and clustering strength.}}
